title: Meridian Brand Identity
description: Full brand identity system for Meridian Coffee Co., a specialty single-origin roaster. Included wordmark, logotype variants, packaging system, and print collateral. The mark draws from compass geometry — precision and direction as metaphors for sourcing.

caseStudy.problem: Meridian needed to differentiate in a saturated specialty coffee market. Their existing identity was generic — nothing communicated their rigorous sourcing philosophy or the craft behind their roasting process.
caseStudy.process: Started with a week of competitive audit and positioning workshops with the client. Developed three identity directions — Cartographic, Elemental, and Structural — before aligning on Cartographic. Built the wordmark from compass geometry, then extended the system into packaging and collateral.
caseStudy.outcome: Launched to strong reception at the 2024 Norfolk Coffee Expo. Wholesale accounts increased 40% in the first quarter following rebrand. The packaging system has since been adopted across their full twelve-SKU product line.

title: Atlas — Editorial Design
description: Art direction and layout for Atlas, an annual architecture and urbanism journal. Designed for long-form reading with a rigorous typographic system built around a single variable typeface at multiple optical sizes.

caseStudy.problem: The journal's prior design prioritized image over text, undermining its identity as a serious critical publication. The editorial team wanted a redesign that signaled depth and rigor without becoming austere.
caseStudy.process: Conducted a full typographic audit and defined a new reading hierarchy using three optical sizes of a single variable serif. Developed a modular grid that could accommodate both dense text and full-bleed imagery. Created a template system usable by the in-house team for future issues.
caseStudy.outcome: The redesigned issue won a Type Directors Club Certificate of Excellence. The template system has been in continuous use for four subsequent issues with no further intervention.

title: Fieldwork — Wayfinding System
description: Environmental wayfinding and signage system for Fieldwork, a 45,000 sq ft co-working and events campus in Norfolk. Designed to guide visitors across three distinct building volumes with differing architectural characters.

caseStudy.problem: Three buildings, three eras of architecture, no unified visual language. Visitors regularly got lost navigating between spaces, and the existing signage was a patchwork of inconsistent vendor solutions.
caseStudy.process: Began with a thorough site survey and behavior mapping — observing how people moved (and got stuck) through the space over two weeks. Developed a system language that could adapt across metal, wood, and glass substrates while remaining visually coherent. Coordinated with fabricators throughout.
caseStudy.outcome: Installed across all three buildings in Q4 2023. Facilities team reported a significant drop in 'lost visitor' front-desk inquiries within the first month. The system language has been adopted as the campus standard for all future signage.

title: Solace Wellness — Brand Identity
description: Brand identity for Solace, a mental health and wellness practice focused on somatic therapy. The system needed to communicate warmth and safety without resorting to the clichés of the wellness industry.

caseStudy.problem: The mental health space is saturated with soft pastels and abstract human figures. Solace needed an identity that felt genuinely considered — warm but precise, approachable but not saccharine.
caseStudy.process: Ran a positioning audit across 40 competitors to identify the visual clichés to avoid. Developed a mark based on overlapping arcs — the idea of two things meeting without merging. Extended into a warm neutral palette and a typographic system built around humanist letterforms.
caseStudy.outcome: Launched across clinic signage, digital scheduling platforms, and print intake materials. Client reported the rebrand significantly improved first-contact comfort ratings in intake surveys.

title: Drift Brewery — Packaging System
description: Can and bottle packaging system for Drift Brewing, a coastal craft brewery specializing in lagers and farmhouse ales. Each SKU references a different navigational instrument — compass, sextant, parallel rule.

caseStudy.problem: Drift's existing packaging was a collection of one-offs with no systemic logic. As their lineup grew, the shelf presence became incoherent and individual labels competed rather than cohered.
caseStudy.process: Established a container-level system: fixed logotype position, fixed color field, variable instrument illustration. Commissioned line illustrations for six instruments, each keyed to a specific beer style. Developed a grid that could flex across 12oz cans, 16oz tallboys, and 750ml bottles.
caseStudy.outcome: System launched with the spring seasonal lineup. Placed in 200+ retail locations across the mid-Atlantic region. Featured in Print Magazine's packaging annual.

title: Signal Magazine — Redesign
description: Full redesign of Signal, a quarterly magazine covering independent music and sound culture. New typographic system, grid, and visual identity for both print and digital editions.

caseStudy.problem: Signal had grown from a zine into a respected publication but its design still read as amateur. The editorial team wanted a redesign that matched the quality of the writing without losing the publication's DIY energy.
caseStudy.process: Spent three weeks with the editorial team understanding how readers engaged with the content — what they read, what they skipped, how they moved through the magazine. Built the new grid from the reading unit up rather than from the page down. Introduced a second typeface for pull quotes and captions to create visual rhythm without decoration.
caseStudy.outcome: Single-copy newsstand sales increased 28% in the issue following the redesign. The template system was handed off to the in-house team with a full style guide and production notes.

title: Compass Architecture — Brand
description: Brand identity and collateral system for Compass, a residential architecture firm with a focus on passive house construction. The identity needed to communicate technical rigor while remaining approachable to homeowner clients.

caseStudy.problem: Architecture firms often default to either corporate sterility or expressive abstraction. Compass needed an identity that communicated both technical precision and genuine warmth toward the families they work with.
caseStudy.process: The mark develops from the floor plan — a plan view of a simple gable form reduced to its essential lines. Extended the system into a document suite, site signage, and a proposal template that the firm now uses for every new project bid.
caseStudy.outcome: The identity has been in use for eighteen months with no patches. The proposal template reduced the firm's bid preparation time by approximately four hours per project.

title: Overture Music Festival — Event Identity
description: Identity system for Overture, a three-day contemporary classical music festival. Included poster series, program booklet, venue signage, and merchandise. The visual language references musical notation systems and score typography.

caseStudy.problem: Contemporary classical music struggles with a perception problem — it reads as inaccessible. Overture wanted an identity that would attract a younger audience without alienating the existing base of serious listeners.
caseStudy.process: Found an unexpected entry point in score notation — the visual language of music is both deeply familiar and, at high magnification, surprisingly abstract and beautiful. Developed a mark and poster series from enlarged score fragments. The program booklet used the same visual logic but remained fully functional as a reading document.
caseStudy.outcome: Festival attendance increased 35% year-over-year. The poster series sold out as limited edition prints within the first day of the festival. The identity system was licensed for use in the second annual event.

title: Pale Horse Press — Book Series
description: Design for a six-volume series of collected essays from an independent literary press. Each volume required its own visual identity within a cohesive series system — unified spine treatment, differentiated covers, shared interior grid.

caseStudy.problem: Series design requires two contradictory things simultaneously: each volume must be immediately recognizable as part of the series, and each must feel like a distinct object worth owning on its own terms.
caseStudy.process: Established a spine system — unified height, typeface, and color band placement — as the series anchor. Freed the cover field entirely so each volume could use a different image approach. Developed an interior grid that could handle both dense essay text and sparse poetry without layout changes.
caseStudy.outcome: All six volumes shipped on schedule. Two titles have gone into second printing. The series received a design merit award from AIGA.

title: Rove — Travel Brand System
description: Brand identity and design system for Rove, a slow-travel platform connecting travelers with week-long stays in working farms, artist residencies, and remote properties. System spans web, app, and print confirmation materials.

caseStudy.problem: The travel platform space is dominated by photography-led interfaces that all look identical. Rove needed to communicate the texture and particularity of its properties — that these were genuinely different kinds of places.
caseStudy.process: Developed a typographic-first identity that let the property descriptions carry the visual weight rather than competing with hero photography. Built a design system with three layout modes — sparse, standard, and dense — so property hosts could choose the presentation that fit their space.
caseStudy.outcome: Platform launched with 120 properties. User time-on-page metrics significantly exceeded industry benchmarks. The typographic approach was specifically cited in press coverage as differentiating the product.

title: Haven Community Center — Wayfinding
description: Comprehensive wayfinding system for Haven, a multi-use community center serving a linguistically diverse neighborhood. System required bilingual implementation across English and Spanish throughout, with accessibility compliance as a primary constraint.

caseStudy.problem: The building served a community where over 40% of users were more comfortable in Spanish than English. Existing signage was English-only and had been supplemented with ad-hoc handwritten translations that created a confusing and unwelcoming environment.
caseStudy.process: Began with community listening sessions to understand how different user groups navigated the building. Developed a bilingual type system where English and Spanish were visually equal — neither translated the other, both were primary. Conducted accessibility review with a specialist consultant at every stage.
caseStudy.outcome: Installed across all four floors. Staff reported an immediate reduction in directional questions at the front desk. The system was cited in a municipal accessibility review as a model for future public building projects.

title: Spoke Bicycle Co. — Brand + Packaging
description: Brand identity and retail packaging system for Spoke, an independent bicycle component manufacturer. The system spans component packaging, shop signage, and a service manual series.

caseStudy.problem: Component packaging in the bicycle industry prioritizes technical spec information at the expense of visual clarity. Spoke wanted packaging that communicated quality at a glance while still satisfying the spec-hungry cyclist.
caseStudy.process: Developed a grid system where the primary face of every package featured only the component name and a technical elevation drawing — no photography, no marketing copy. All spec information moved to the reverse. Extended the elevation drawing approach into the service manual series.
caseStudy.outcome: Retail partners reported the packaging was the most frequently commented-on aspect of the product launch. The service manual series was made available as a free digital download and has been viewed over 80,000 times.

title: Lumen Studio — Identity
description: Brand identity for Lumen, an architectural photography studio. The system needed to recede entirely — to frame the work without competing with it. Monochromatic, typographic, systematic.

caseStudy.problem: Photography studio identities routinely make the mistake of being visually interesting in the wrong way — the logo and brand compete with the photographs for attention. Lumen's work was exceptional; the identity needed to get out of the way.
caseStudy.process: The solution is almost a non-design: a single weight of a single typeface, set in a single size, with strict rules about its placement relative to images. The 'system' is a set of constraints rather than a set of assets.
caseStudy.outcome: The studio launched with the new identity and attributed a measurable improvement in initial client inquiry quality — clients were responding to the work, not to the studio's visual presence.

title: Mineral Skincare — Packaging
description: Packaging system for Mineral, a skincare line built around single-ingredient formulations. The packaging needed to communicate the brand's radical simplicity — one ingredient, one bottle, no complexity.

caseStudy.problem: The skincare industry defaults to aspirational photography, ingredient lists in small type, and visual complexity as a proxy for efficacy. Mineral's value proposition was the opposite — radical simplicity — and the packaging needed to embody that.
caseStudy.process: Developed packaging where the single ingredient name is set in the largest possible type the container allows. No photography. No ingredient list beyond the single ingredient. The label is almost entirely typographic. Tested fifteen paper stocks before selecting an uncoated stock that communicates tactile quality without decoration.
caseStudy.outcome: Placed in 45 specialty retail locations within three months of launch. Featured in a major retail publication's 'best packaging' roundup. Reorder rate significantly exceeded projections in the first quarter.

title: Watershed Film Festival — Identity
description: Annual identity for Watershed, an environmental documentary film festival. The system includes poster series, program, venue signage, digital assets, and a motion opening title sequence for screenings.

caseStudy.problem: Environmental causes are frequently represented through obvious imagery — green palettes, leaf motifs, water. Watershed needed an identity that took the subject matter seriously without resorting to environmental design clichés.
caseStudy.process: Found a more interesting entry in the idea of the document itself — scientific field notes, survey drawings, tide charts. Developed an identity that referenced this visual language of observation and record rather than advocacy. The motion title sequence used archival survey footage as its source material.
caseStudy.outcome: Festival attendance grew 22% year-over-year. The poster series received independent recognition in both design and environmental communication contexts — an unusual dual reception that the festival director described as exactly the right signal.

title: Silt Restaurant Group — Brand
description: Brand identity and collateral for Silt, a restaurant group operating three distinct concepts — a seafood counter, a bakery, and a natural wine bar. Each concept required its own visual identity within a shared parent system.

caseStudy.problem: Multi-concept restaurant groups need parent identity logic that holds the concepts together without homogenizing them. Each of Silt's three concepts had a distinct customer and a distinct tone — the design system needed to respect that.
caseStudy.process: Built a parent system based on a single structural element — a horizontal rule — that appears in each concept's identity at different weights and densities. The seafood counter uses thin rules and generous space; the wine bar uses dense, stacked rules; the bakery sits between them. The typefaces are concept-specific; the structural logic is shared.
caseStudy.outcome: All three locations have opened. The identities read as related but distinct — press and customers consistently describe the relationship correctly without having it explained to them.

title: Parallax Digital — Design System
description: Comprehensive design system for Parallax, a B2B analytics platform. The system spans a component library of 140+ components, a token architecture, documentation site, and a Figma library for the product design team.

caseStudy.problem: Parallax had grown from a startup to a 60-person product organization with design debt accumulating faster than it could be addressed. Components were inconsistent across product surfaces, and every new feature required solving the same layout problems from scratch.
caseStudy.process: Audited the existing product to identify and categorize all component patterns. Developed a token architecture grounded in the brand before building any components. Built components in order of product frequency, not visual complexity. Wrote documentation for every component — not just what it is, but when to use it and when not to.
caseStudy.outcome: Design system shipped to the product team over six months. Engineering velocity improved measurably in the first quarter after adoption. New feature development time decreased by an estimated 30% based on team reporting.

title: Cairn Outdoor Gear — Brand System
description: Brand identity for Cairn, a small-batch outdoor gear manufacturer focused on ultralight backpacking equipment. The identity needed to communicate precision engineering without sacrificing the warmth of a human-scale business.

caseStudy.problem: The outdoor gear industry has two modes — mass-market athleticism and premium heritage. Cairn occupied neither; they made highly engineered small-batch gear for serious backcountry travelers who distrust both corporate scale and affected nostalgia.
caseStudy.process: Developed an identity based on the technical drawing — the same precise linework used in gear specification documents. The mark is a cairn constructed from minimal geometric forms. Extended into a product catalog that reads like a technical report: no lifestyle photography, component diagrams, material specs in the foreground.
caseStudy.outcome: Brand launched with the spring gear line. Pre-orders exceeded projections by 60%. The catalog approach generated significant press attention, with several publications specifically noting the departure from category conventions.

title: Harrow Type — Specimen Design
description: Printed type specimen for Harrow, a new text typeface family from an independent foundry. The specimen needed to demonstrate the typeface's performance across a full range of editorial contexts — body text, display, caption, tabular.

caseStudy.problem: Type specimens are often either purely technical — showing the alphabet at various sizes — or purely decorative — showing the typeface set in beautiful but artificial contexts. Neither tells a designer what they actually need to know: how does this typeface perform when used in real work?
caseStudy.process: Structured the specimen as a simulated publication — a mock journal of essays — so every setting demonstrates the typeface in the kind of document it would actually be used in. Technical information is present throughout but integrated into the editorial layout rather than separated into a reference section.
caseStudy.outcome: The specimen was distributed to 800 designers and type directors. The foundry reported a 4x increase in license sales in the month following distribution, attributing the spike directly to the specimen's success in demonstrating real-world performance.

title: Archive Records — Album Art Series
description: Album artwork for a series of six reissues from Archive Records, an independent label releasing archival electronic music. Each sleeve required both period authenticity and contemporary visual quality.

caseStudy.problem: Archival reissue design involves a difficult balance: the artwork should honor the period of the original recording without being a pastiche of it. The label wanted sleeves that felt archival but were clearly designed now, by someone who understood both eras.
caseStudy.process: Researched the graphic conventions of the original recording periods — early 1980s European electronic music — and identified which elements were genuinely structural versus which were merely stylistic artifacts. Rebuilt the structural elements using contemporary tools, deliberately setting aside the stylistic markers.
caseStudy.outcome: The series shipped to 300 independent record stores globally. Two sleeves were included in a year-end 'best reissue packaging' list in a major music publication. The label commissioned a second series of four titles.

title: Verdant Garden Supply — Brand
description: Brand identity and retail print system for Verdant, an independent garden supply store with a focus on native plants and soil health. The identity references botanical illustration as a formal language without quoting it directly.

caseStudy.problem: Garden retail identities almost universally use illustration or photography of plants in predictable ways. Verdant wanted to occupy the category differently — the store was for serious gardeners, not casual plant buyers, and the identity needed to communicate that seriousness.
caseStudy.process: Found a more interesting entry in the visual language of the garden notebook — plant tags, hand-drawn plans, soil sample charts. Developed a mark from botanical classification structure: the Latin binomial, set in a precise hierarchical arrangement. Extended into a paper bag series and a seed catalog.
caseStudy.outcome: Store traffic increased 18% in the first quarter after the identity launched. The seed catalog became an unexpected driver of repeat visits — customers came in specifically to collect each issue.

title: Northlight Photography — Portfolio
description: Portfolio book and website for Northlight, an architectural photographer. The design system needed to create a consistent context for the work across print and digital without imposing its own visual personality.

caseStudy.problem: Architectural photography portfolio design has a fundamental tension: the designer needs to create a strong frame while making themselves invisible. Too little design and the work floats without context; too much and the design competes.
caseStudy.process: The solution is constraint rather than design: a fixed margin system, a single weight of a single typeface for all metadata, and a strict rule that no more than one image appears per spread in the print book. The website uses the same logic — single image per page, fixed caption position, no visual noise.
caseStudy.outcome: The book was used in presentations to three major architecture firms within its first year. Two of those presentations resulted in commissions. The photographer has described the portfolio as the most effective marketing tool they've used.

title: Fern & Fig — Retail Identity
description: Brand identity and environmental graphics for Fern & Fig, an independent houseplant and ceramics shop. The identity needed to work across storefront signage, printed materials, and a small e-commerce presence.

caseStudy.problem: Independent retail has an identity problem: most small shops use either generic templates or self-designed materials that communicate charm but not quality. Fern & Fig wanted an identity that communicated genuine craft at every touchpoint.
caseStudy.process: Developed the mark from letterpress printing conventions — a process that imposed useful constraints on color and weight. The identity uses two colors maximum at every scale, which forced decisions that are cleaner than a full-color approach would have allowed. Extended into tissue paper, hang tags, and a simple e-commerce template.
caseStudy.outcome: Store opened with the identity in place. The brand received unsolicited press coverage from two local publications, both specifically noting the design quality. The shop's Instagram following grew organically without paid promotion, largely through customer posts of the packaging.

title: Crane Paper Co. — Identity
description: Brand identity and stationery system for Crane, a small paper goods manufacturer producing letterpress and offset materials for independent retailers. The identity was designed to live primarily on paper — no digital application required.

caseStudy.problem: A paper company's identity is also a demonstration of its product. Every printed piece is simultaneously communication and sample — the design had to function as both simultaneously.
caseStudy.process: Designed the identity specifically to exploit the characteristics of uncoated paper: debossed impressions, blind embossing, and a color palette that behaves differently on coated versus uncoated stock. The system includes a sample pack that introduces each paper type through an application designed for that stock.
caseStudy.outcome: The identity has been in use for three years without modification. The sample pack is used as the primary sales tool for wholesale accounts. Several retailers have cited the sample pack as the reason they placed their first order.

title: Commonground Co-op — Brand
description: Brand identity for Commonground, a worker-owned food cooperative. The identity needed to communicate cooperative ownership and community values without clichéd communal imagery.

caseStudy.problem: Cooperative and community organizations often use visual languages — hands, circles, shared forms — that communicate their values so broadly that they communicate nothing specific. Commonground wanted an identity that was genuinely theirs.
caseStudy.process: The mark comes from the building's original floor plan — a specific place, belonging to specific people. The typography is set in a face with worker-typesetting history. The color palette is drawn from the building's existing paint and materials rather than chosen abstractly.
caseStudy.outcome: The identity launched with the cooperative's remodel and expansion. Member-owners reported strong identification with the mark once its provenance was explained in the member newsletter. The mark has become genuinely local — appearing in murals and independent merchandise not commissioned by the organization.

title: Kestrel Films — Brand + Motion
description: Brand identity and motion graphics package for Kestrel, an independent documentary film production company. The system spans production logo animation, lower third templates, and a print identity for proposals and press materials.

caseStudy.problem: Film production company identities need to perform in two very different contexts: as a mark that appears on screen at the beginning of a film, and as a static identity on paper in pitch and press contexts. Most production companies solve one and ignore the other.
caseStudy.process: Built the mark to function as an animation premise as well as a static form. The kestrel's form at rest and in motion — the distinctive wingbeat hover — gave the animation a natural logic rather than a designed one. Extended the same logic into lower thirds and title cards.
caseStudy.outcome: The production logo has appeared in festival screenings at Sundance and DOC NYC. The print materials have been credited by the company's principals with improving success rates in initial distributor conversations.

title: Blackwood Publishing — Series Design
description: Interior and cover design for Blackwood's annual short fiction anthology. The commission spans both cover design — which changes each year — and interior typographic system — which remains constant across all editions.

caseStudy.problem: Annual anthology design faces an unusual challenge: the interior needs absolute consistency year over year — readers move between editions, and visual consistency creates a reading continuity that honors the collected work — while the cover needs to announce that this is a new thing.
caseStudy.process: Developed an interior system that is intentionally invisible: a well-spaced text face, a conservative but functional grid, chapter openings that use the typography rather than adding decoration. Treated the cover as a separate brief each year — different art direction, different image approach, unified only by format and logotype placement.
caseStudy.outcome: The series is now in its fifth year with the same interior system. Two of the annual covers have been recognized in design competitions. Several contributors have cited the interior design as unusually respectful of the text.

title: Mariner Seafood — Brand Identity
description: Brand identity for Mariner, a community-supported fishery connecting local fishing boats directly with household subscribers. The identity needed to communicate freshness, locality, and the working-boat character of the operation.

caseStudy.problem: Seafood branding exists in two registers: the premium restaurant supplier with its dark backgrounds and gold type, and the cheerful fish-market with its cartoon fish and bright primary colors. Neither was right for a CSF focused on transparency and community relationship.
caseStudy.process: Found the right tone in the visual language of the working waterfront: nautical charts, catch logs, boat registration documents. Developed an identity that read as functional rather than decorative — as if the brand had been made by the fishermen rather than for them.
caseStudy.outcome: Subscriber base grew from 180 to 650 households in the year following the rebrand. The identity has been adopted by three other regional CSFs with permission as a model for their own rebrands.

title: Latitude — Publication Design
description: Art direction and design for Latitude, a biannual journal of geography, cartography, and place. Each issue is organized around a single theme and designed as a unified reading experience from cover to last page.

caseStudy.problem: Academic journals prioritize information density over reading experience, while general-interest magazines sacrifice rigor for approachability. Latitude wanted to occupy neither — a serious publication that was also genuinely pleasurable to read.
caseStudy.process: Treated each issue as a designed object with a beginning, middle, and end — not a collection of discrete articles. Developed pacing through section breaks, image placement, and type scale changes that create a reading rhythm across the full issue. Worked closely with the editor to understand the conceptual arc of each issue before beginning design.
caseStudy.outcome: Subscription base has grown steadily over four issues without paid promotion. Two issues have sold through their initial print runs. The publication has been cited in several design contexts as an example of rigorous editorial design applied to academic content.

title: Horizon Conference — Identity
description: Identity system for Horizon, an annual design and technology conference. The system includes registration materials, wayfinding, badge design, speaker slide templates, and a motion identity for live presentation.

caseStudy.problem: Conference identity design is usually applied to materials after the programming decisions have been made. Horizon gave us the unusual opportunity to develop the identity in conversation with the programming — to build a visual language that reflected the actual content of the event.
caseStudy.process: The conference theme was productive disagreement — how to hold different perspectives simultaneously. The identity used a two-column grid system throughout, always presenting two things in parallel: two typefaces, two colors, two perspectives. Speakers were asked to frame their talks as explicit pairs.
caseStudy.outcome: Attendance reached 1,200 for the first time in the conference's history. The identity was widely reproduced on social media during the event. Three speakers specifically incorporated the two-column framing into their talk structure in response to the brief.

title: Tidal Press — Identity + Collateral
description: Brand identity and collateral system for Tidal Press, an independent publisher focused on maritime history and coastal ecology. System includes logo, stationery, catalog, and spine treatment standards for the backlist.

caseStudy.problem: Independent publishers often have no visual coherence across their list — each book is designed in isolation, and the press has no presence as an entity distinct from its titles. Tidal needed an identity that gave the press a recognizable character without homogenizing the books.
caseStudy.process: Developed a press identity built around consistency of spine treatment — color, typeface, and proportion — so the backlist reads as a collection on a shelf. Released the cover field entirely to per-title art direction. The stationery system uses the same typographic rigor as the spine treatment.
caseStudy.outcome: The backlist spine system has been applied retroactively to twelve existing titles. Booksellers have cited the unified spine treatment as improving the press's shelf presence significantly. The catalog has been used successfully as a trade sales tool.

title: Thrift & Ride
description: Campaign concept and art direction for the launch of the adidas Forum '84 'Forum by New York City' colorway. A repurposed vintage NYC transit bus, wrapped in a custom Forum livery and outfitted as a mobile thrift shop, served as the campaign's hero environment — connecting the silhouette's basketball-court heritage to a contemporary NYC street and secondhand-style culture. Photography captured the bus exterior, styled interior, product, packaging, and a cast of New York creatives shopping the rolling thrift floor. [NEEDS REVIEW]

caseStudy.problem: [NEEDS REVIEW] Placeholder brief in notes.txt — confirm the campaign objective. Working assumption: launch a heritage-court silhouette into a contemporary NYC streetwear context without leaning on the usual NBA-throwback playbook, and give the colorway a tangible cultural moment beyond a standard product drop.
caseStudy.process: [NEEDS REVIEW] Built the campaign around a single hero object — a wrapped vintage city bus styled as a rolling Forum-branded thrift shop. Developed the bus livery, interior set design, custom packaging (hangtag, dust bag, instax-ready insert), branded tote, lookbook, and cast styling. Directed a photo shoot on 2021-06-28 capturing the bus on-site at a graffiti-covered warehouse, the styled interior, on-foot street moments, and product/packaging flatlays.
caseStudy.outcome: [NEEDS REVIEW] Confirm launch metrics, press coverage, awards, and any retail/sell-through outcomes. Two finished video edits exist (16:9 30MB and 16:9 YouTube cuts) — flag for inclusion once schema supports video.
